items.html:
Why a speak-up culture matters

Whistleblowing is not about disloyalty. When workers feel able to speak up, poor care is caught early and people are kept safe. When they stay silent, harm can continue for years. Some of the most serious failings in English care were allowed to grow because staff did not feel able to raise the alarm, or raised concerns and were ignored.

At Mid Staffordshire, and at Winterbourne View — a hospital for people with learning disabilities — warning signs went unheard until far too late. The lesson is not that individual workers were to blame, but that a culture where speaking up feels risky puts everyone in danger.

Freedom to Speak Up

Following these failings, the Freedom to Speak Up review set out how organisations should make it normal and safe for staff to raise concerns. The aim is a culture where speaking up is welcomed, listened to and acted on — not treated as making trouble.

  • Openness: concerns are seen as a gift that helps care improve.
  • Listening: managers take what you say seriously and feed back what happens next.
  • Learning: the focus is on putting things right, not finding someone to punish.
  • Everyone’s role: a healthy culture depends on each worker being willing to speak.

items.question: What is the main lesson from care failings such as Mid Staffordshire and Winterbourne View?

answers.text: A culture where staff feel unable to speak up lets harm continue, so speaking up must be made safe and normal.
answers.feedback: Correct. The failings show why organisations must build a culture where raising concerns is welcomed and acted on.

answers.text: Whistleblowers usually exaggerate, so their concerns should be treated with caution.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The failings happened because genuine concerns were dismissed, not because staff exaggerated.

answers.text: Raising concerns rarely changes anything, so it is better to wait and see.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. These cases show the opposite: silence allowed harm to continue, while speaking up could have stopped it sooner.

answers.text: Individual care workers are always to blame when standards slip.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The reviews focused on unsafe cultures and ignored warnings, not on blaming individual workers.

items.html:
What you might need to raise

Whistleblowing covers serious wrongdoing that harms, or risks harming, the people you support, your colleagues or the public. It is broader than a single act of poor care. If something feels seriously wrong, it is better to raise it than to assume someone else already has.

Concerns worth raising include:

  • Poor care: neglect, people left in pain, or care given without dignity.
  • Abuse: physical, sexual, emotional, financial or discriminatory harm.
  • Unsafe practice: dangerous staffing levels, missed medication, or ignored procedures.

Wrongdoing beyond the bedside

Not every concern is about hands-on care. Whistleblowing also covers dishonesty and danger in how a service is run.

  • Fraud: false records, stolen money, or claiming for care that was never given.
  • Cover-ups: hiding a mistake, altering notes, or silencing anyone who complains.
  • Health and safety dangers: broken equipment, fire hazards or serious infection risks.

You do not need proof, and you do not need to be certain. If you have a reasonable, honest belief that something is wrong, that is enough to raise it — investigating is someone else’s job, not yours.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about what you can raise.
items.textField: Whistleblowing covers dishonesty such as *fraud*, where money or records are falsified. Hiding a mistake or altering notes is a *cover-up*. You do not need *proof* before you raise a concern. A reasonable, honest *belief* that something is wrong is enough to speak up. Broken equipment and fire hazards are *safety* dangers worth reporting.

items.html:
The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998

The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998, often shortened to PIDA, is the law that protects workers who blow the whistle. It does not protect every complaint — it protects a “qualifying disclosure”, which becomes a protected disclosure when it is made in the right way.

To qualify, you must have a reasonable belief that your information shows serious wrongdoing: a criminal offence, a failure to meet a legal duty, a danger to someone’s health or safety, damage to the environment, or an attempt to hide any of these.

Public interest, good faith and tiers

Your disclosure must also be in the public interest — about protecting others, not settling a personal grievance. Since the law was updated in 2013 you no longer have to prove you acted in “good faith”, though acting honestly still matters and bad faith can reduce any award you might receive.

The law recognises tiers of disclosure:

  • To your employer: usually the first and easiest step.
  • To a prescribed person: a named body such as the CQC.
  • Wider disclosure: for example to the media, allowed only in limited and stricter circumstances.

items.question: Under PIDA, when does a concern become a “protected disclosure”?

answers.text: When you have a reasonable belief it shows serious wrongdoing and you raise it in the public interest through the right channel.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reasonable belief, a public-interest purpose and the right route together make a disclosure protected.

answers.text: Only when you can prove the wrongdoing beyond doubt before you report it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You need a reasonable belief, not cast-iron proof; investigating is not your job.

answers.text: Only if you report it straight to the media so the public hears about it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Wider disclosure to the media is only protected in limited circumstances; usually you start with your employer or a prescribed person.

answers.text: Automatically, whenever you complain about anything at work.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A personal grievance is not the same as a public-interest disclosure about serious wrongdoing.

items.html:
What holds people back

Most care workers who stay silent are not uncaring — they are held back by very real feelings. Recognising these barriers is the first step to overcoming them.

  • Fear: worry about losing your job, being bullied or being blamed.
  • Loyalty: not wanting to get a colleague or your employer into trouble.
  • Doubt: thinking you might be wrong, or that no one will believe you.
  • Habit: assuming “that is just how things are done here”.

Where to find support

You do not have to carry a concern alone. Remember that the law protects you from being treated badly for speaking up in the public interest, and there are people who can help you decide what to do.

  • Your manager: usually the quickest way to get a concern acted on.
  • The CQC: a prescribed person you can contact if raising it internally has not worked.
  • Protect: a whistleblowing charity offering free, confidential advice.
  • Your union: can offer advice and representation if you are a member.

items.question: If you feel unable to raise a concern with your employer, an independent charity such as Protect can give you free, confidential advice about your options.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Protect is a whistleblowing charity that offers free, confidential advice to help you decide how to raise a concern safely.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Protect is a whistleblowing charity that offers free, confidential advice, so you do have somewhere independent to turn.

items.html:
What happens after you raise a concern

Speaking up is the start of a process, not the end. Knowing roughly what to expect can make it feel less daunting.

  • Acknowledgement: the person you told should confirm they have heard you.
  • Assessment: your concern is looked at and, where needed, investigated.
  • Action: steps are taken to make people safe and put things right.
  • Feedback: you should be told the outcome as far as is possible.

Throughout, keep your own dated notes of what you saw, who you told and when.

A realistic scenario

You notice a colleague regularly signs the medication chart before residents have actually taken their tablets. You feel torn — you like her, and you dread being seen as a troublemaker.

You decide to act. You write down the dates and exactly what you saw, then raise it with your manager the same day. Your manager thanks you, keeps your name confidential, and starts a review. Because you spoke up, a serious risk to residents is stopped early — and your careful records show you acted honestly and in the public interest.

items.question: In the scenario, what makes your decision to raise the concern effective and well protected?

answers.text: You kept dated records, raised it promptly with your manager, and acted honestly in the public interest.
answers.feedback: Correct. Prompt reporting, honest motives and clear records give your disclosure the best protection.

answers.text: You waited several weeks to be completely certain before saying anything.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Waiting for certainty leaves residents at risk; a reasonable belief is enough to raise it now.

answers.text: You confronted your colleague alone and agreed to keep it between the two of you.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Keeping it private with the person involved does not protect residents or you; the concern needs to reach your manager.

answers.text: You posted about it on social media so that others would be warned.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Public disclosure is only protected in limited circumstances and breaches confidentiality; raise it internally first.
